Its 1983 election platform—promising centralized economic planning, unilateral nuclear disarmament, and the introduction of a price-control commission—was so extreme that critics dubbed it “the longest suicide note in history.” After the party lost in a historic landslide to the Conservatives, major factions within Labour still insisted that the party wasn’t left-wing enough. The Labour politician Edward “Red Ted” Knight famously declared that “there can be no compromise with the electorate.”

One small but loud faction on Labour’s left was the Militant Tendency, a highly disciplined group that took its name from the socialist newspaper around which its members gathered. Giant banners showing the faces of Karl Marx, Friedrich Engels, Vladimir Lenin, and Leon Trotsky adorned its rallies. Unlike the Labour mainstream, which sought to alleviate inequality through democratic reform, Militant believed in the overthrow of British capitalism, by force if necessary. With its own manifestos, conferences, leadership structures, and funding, Militant resembled a traditional political party in every respect but one: It didn’t run candidates on a ballot line of its own. In a ploy that might sound familiar to Democrats in the United States today, Militant instructed its members to join Labour, ascend its ranks, and advance the group’s cause from the inside. By the time Kinnock assumed leadership of the Labour Party after the 1983 disaster, Militant had been functioning as “a party within a party” for some two decades.

At Labour’s annual conference in Bournemouth, England, in 1985, Kinnock’s frustration boiled over. He spoke emotionally about how he owed the Labour Party “every life chance that I have had since the time I was a child,” and argued that far-left purists’ “impossible promises” and commitment to theories over results imperiled Labour’s ability to protect the welfare state. He denounced Militant members as “gesture generals” and likened them to privately educated schoolboys who were willing to “play politics with people’s jobs and with people’s services or with their homes.” Militant members and their fellow travelers booed, and a former Labour chairman stormed off the stage in anger. But Kinnock had the courage to say what many in Labour were thinking. Most of the crowd roared their approval.

In a television appearance the following month, Kinnock went further, denouncing Militant as “a maggot in the body of the Labour Party.” Despite fierce internal resistance, Kinnock ended up expelling hundreds of Militant members. More than four decades later, Kinnock’s actions remain among the most memorable examples of a political leader confronting extremists on his own side.

Kinnock was among his generation’s greatest political orators—to the point that Joe Biden, then a senator from Delaware, plagiarized some of his most effective lines. Although Kinnock never managed to become prime minister, he saved the Labour Party from electoral oblivion. He paved the way for a successor, Tony Blair, to steer the party back into power in 1997 and become the only Labour leader to win three successive elections.

Today, Democratic leaders find themselves in a quandary much like Kinnock’s. The DSA’s agenda is alien to mainstream Democrats—or at least it should be. The organization’s draft platform calls for “fully abolishing the police and prison system,” drafting a “new constitution,” extending voting rights to noncitizens, and creating a “democratic socialist republic.” The group holds the U.S. and NATO responsible for Russia’s war against Ukraine, defends the Communist dictatorship in Cuba, and pledges to shut down the Department of Defense and all overseas American military bases.

To gain leverage within the Democratic Party, the DSA is employing a tactic that Trotsky dubbed “entryism”—the willful effort by a small, disciplined cadre of radicals to infiltrate a more moderate organization and subvert it from within. It’s the same tactic that, throughout history, far-left organizations such as the Militant Tendency have routinely used to amass power. Unless the Democrats respond forcefully to the DSA’s entryism, they risk being overrun by extremists who will poison the party’s national brand and sap its capacity to compete with an already radicalized GOP.

In liberal enclaves, the DSA keeps getting stronger. Last month in Washington, D.C., a DSA candidate beat the outgoing mayor’s chosen successor, and this fall in Los Angeles, a DSA city-council member could unseat the incumbent Democratic mayor. Recently, two current members and one former member of the DSA—all endorsed by the group’s most prominent elected official, New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani—defeated establishment Democrats, including the chairman of the House Hispanic Caucus, in congressional primaries. Since his inauguration, Mamdani has shown a moderate streak. One of the insurgents, a doctoral student in sociology named Darializa Avila Chevalier, is far more emblematic of the DSA’s radicalism. She has called for the abolition of borders, police, and prisons; has endorsed the seizure of private property; opposes deportations on any grounds; and took part in a DSA-supported pro-Hamas rally the day after the October 7 massacre. Recently in Colorado, a progressive 15-term Democratic member of Congress lost a primary election to a DSA candidate who refuses to condemn as anti-Semitic the firebombing of a demonstration for Israeli hostages.

These victories mark major milestones for the DSA’s overall political strategy. Knowing that it doesn’t have anything near the support to compete as a party in its own right, the DSA opportunistically uses the Democratic Party ballot line (which some members describe as a “tool”) to get its candidates elected. The DSA declined to endorse the Democratic nominee in the past three presidential elections, and members attack the Democratic Party’s leaders as neoliberal corporate shills who must be overthrown. (When images of House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries appeared on television screens at the party celebrating the DSA slate’s win in New York, the audience erupted into chants of “You’re next!”) The DSA is not shy about its ultimate designs. A recent draft platform explicitly called for the eventual formation of an “independent political party, rooted in the working-class majority”—language that was eventually scrubbed.

In fact, little about the DSA’s membership of more than 100,000 people suggests any natural affinity or appeal to America’s multiracial, geographically dispersed working class. A 2021 survey of DSA members found that only 8 percent of respondents lived in rural areas (as opposed to 20 percent of the American public), that more than 80 percent had a college degree (twice the proportion among Americans overall), and that 85 percent were white. The Democrats’ broad rainbow coalition looks much more like America than the DSA does.

The DSA claims, against all evidence, that pulling the Democratic Party to the left will expand its electoral appeal. To moderate-Democratic critics, the group’s recent victories in New York City and elsewhere demonstrate little more than an ability to turn safe Democratic seats an even deeper shade of blue. “I’m not excited about candidates who can turn blue seats navy blue, cobalt blue, or midnight blue,” Rahm Emanuel, the former head of the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee and a possible 2028 presidential contender, told me. In 2006, Emanuel helped the party pick up 30 House seats from Republicans; neither the DSA nor the Bernie Sanders–aligned entities Our Revolution and Justice Democrats have flipped a single one.

Yet despite the faction’s inability to win in purple or red districts, democratic socialists enjoy a net favorability of 17 points among Democratic voters nationwide, compared with just 4 points for Democratic members of Congress. More Democratic voters view socialism favorably than those who say the same of capitalism, 66 percent to 42. Socialism is an expansive term, applicable both to Scandinavian social democracies with strong private sectors and to authoritarian Communist regimes. The DSA includes advocates of both models, allowing hard-liners within the group to pass off their own dogmatic and illiberal ideology as merely a more assertive version of progressivism.

One of the less remarked upon adverse effects of the Trump presidency is the way in which it has radicalized the left, giving license to bad actors and bad ideas. Fearful of appearing weak in the eyes of their base, most elected Democrats have adopted a “no enemies on the left” approach to dealing with the DSA and other radical forces. If such pusillanimity continues, Democrats risk losing their party to a band of true believers no less destructive than the furthest extremes of the MAGA movement.

The DSA, founded in 1982 by the writer and activist Michael Harrington to achieve “the left wing of the possible,” served mainly as a discussion club until 2016, when Sanders’s first presidential campaign inspired a groundswell of interest in socialism. The starkest divide between the DSA’s older generation and the newcomers concerned their attitudes toward democracy. The DSA’s founders drew a bright line between the democratic and authoritarian left, not least because socialists are typically among the first to be put against the wall whenever Communists come to power. A staunch anti-communist and a Zionist, Harrington had no difficulty condemning the human-rights abuses perpetrated by left-wing regimes and Islamist terrorist groups.

To the DSA’s growing ranks of young members, however, Harrington’s anti-communism and Zionism were imperialist, reactionary relics of the Cold War. Maurice Isserman, a founding member of the DSA, has written that by around 2019, “‘Harringtonite’ was becoming a term of abuse within the organization.” As the DSA’s national profile has risen, the group has drawn new members for whom the democratic in democratic socialism is not a core value but an obstacle. Outside activists who abide by the Leninist principle of “democratic centralism”—in which the rank and file must unquestioningly carry out political decisions made by party elite—gained entry into the DSA. The group “became a sort of magnet for people who had a different sort of sectarian view of what the left should be,” Leo Casey, another founding DSA member, told me. Before the DSA became a perpetrator of entryism, it was the victim of it.

Then came October 7. The DSA had officially repudiated its Zionist past in 2017 when it endorsed the Boycott, Divestment, and Sanctions movement against Israel. But even this hostile move did not prepare the DSA’s older membership for the virulent anti-Semitism that would pour forth from the group after the worst mass murder of Jews since the Holocaust. The DSA released a statement “expressing our solidarity with Palestine” with no mention of Hamas, the terrorist outfit that had perpetrated the massacre. The following day, the New York City chapter endorsed a pro-Hamas rally in Times Square—the rally that the future Democratic congressional nominee Chevalier attended. The group’s embrace of terrorist violence proved too much for some members. “Sunday’s hate-filled and antisemitic rally in New York City, promoted by the NYC-DSA, makes it impossible for me to continue my affiliation,” Representative Shri Thanedar of Michigan, a Democrat, said in a statement. (For this heresy, Thanedar is at risk of losing his seat to a DSA-backed challenger.) In Los Angeles, City Councilmember Nithya Raman, a DSA member, said the organization’s statement “failed to reckon with the horrors committed by Hamas” and was “devoid of empathy.” When she later accepted support from a local pro-Israel organization, the DSA’s Los Angeles chapter censured her, noting that its elected officials “are expected to co-govern with the chapter,” and it continues to withhold its endorsement in her challenge to Los Angeles Mayor Karen Bass. At the DSA convention last August, the group repealed its long-standing ban on members from democratic-centralist organizations, effectively opening the door to Communists, who now form a majority on its governing body.

To win an endorsement from the New York City DSA, prospective candidates must pass through a rigorous vetting process. Hopefuls must appear before multiple committees to ensure that they’re sufficiently simpatico with DSA diktats. Those who receive the DSA’s red rose of approval are subject to draconian ideological monitoring. In 2024, the group’s national political committee revoked its endorsement of Representative Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, then its most famous member, over her support for the Iron Dome missile-defense system in Israel and sponsorship of an event with a Jewish organization focused on combatting anti-Semitism. An article in the group’s official publication complained that she was “committed to the Democratic Party, not the DSA,” and a faction demanded that she be censured for her “tacit support of Zionism,” her endorsement of Joe Biden and Kamala Harris, and—in a Stalinist flourish—her “inadequate political discipline.”

“The trouble with socialism,” according to an old joke often misattributed to Oscar Wilde, “is that it takes up too many evenings.” The DSA’s supporters and critics both agree that its members devote enormous time and energy to on-the-ground organizing. Representative Tom Suozzi, a moderate Democrat from Long Island, lamented to me that “people who oppose the DSA are at cocktail parties wringing their hands while they’re out there organizing.” For this reason, among others, some Democrats see DSA members as potential allies. “DSA members are doing the one thing Democrats can barely do anymore, especially at the local level, which is organize,” Steven McCarty, a District of Columbia Democratic Party committee member and Democratic Municipal Officials board member, told me. In this view, the DSA is a resource that should be integrated into the Democratic Party, with all of the ideological compromises such a merger would necessitate. Many senior Democrats are thinking along the same lines. Jeffries has refrained from criticizing the new DSA congressional nominees despite their conspicuous refusal to endorse him for the speakership. Axios recently reported that Harris, eyeing another run for the presidency, had called Mamdani “to talk about the party’s future.”

Other Democrats see more clearly what’s wrong with accommodating the DSA. Emanuel told me that although the Democrats are a coalition, “the coalition doesn’t include room for people who believe America is a country wrong at every level.” Representative Josh Gottheimer of New Jersey, a prominent moderate Democrat and vocal DSA critic, likened his party’s situation to that of the GOP more than a decade ago. “I see this as our Tea Party / Freedom Caucus moment,” he told me, with the Democratic Party staring down a movement “interested in bomb throwing, not problem solving; sowing chaos, not finding solutions.” Last month, Suozzi and Representative Adam Gray of California released an open letter that proclaims, “We are capitalist, not socialist,” “We want safety, not lawlessness,” and “We are proud, not ashamed of America.” As of this writing, only eight other members of Congress have signed the pledge.

Democrats can look across the aisle to see what a vigorous contest for the soul of a major political party looks like. Given how slavishly the GOP has fallen into line behind Trump, it’s hard to remember the ferocity of the ideological battles that took place within the conservative movement while he was attempting a hostile takeover of that party in 2015 and 2016. The conservative-activist-and-intellectual space was galvanized by debates over Trump and what he represented, and a whopping 16 other candidates competed against Trump for the party’s nomination. One of those contenders, former Texas Governor Rick Perry, called Trump’s campaign a “cancer on conservatism,” and another, Texas Senator Ted Cruz, told delegates to “vote your conscience” at the Republican convention. Senator Lindsey Graham called Trump a “race-baiting, xenophobic, religious bigot” who should “go to hell.”

Thus far, the American left is not having any significant debate on the disturbing rise of the DSA. To save their party, and American liberalism, Democrats will need to reach back into their own history, to the early years of the Cold War, when liberal titans such as Arthur Schlesinger Jr. sought to purge the Democratic Party of Communist influence. They will need to understand that the DSA is not, as some Democrats once affectionately described Communists, a collection of well-intentioned if naive “liberals in a hurry,” but an illiberal vanguard. Establishment figures such as Jeffries and the party’s 2028 presidential candidates will need to grasp that the relationship between the DSA and the Democratic Party is not one of occasionally bickering siblings, but of parasite and host.

Democrats have spent the Trump era telling the American people that they’re the only political force capable of defending the country from authoritarianism. That argument will be impossible to maintain if they countenance an organization that honors the legacy of the convicted cop killer Assata Shakur and calls for “solidarity” with North Korea. But criticizing the DSA will only go so far. When David Duke was the Republican candidate for governor in Louisiana in 1991, prominent Republicans, including President George H. W. Bush, condemned the former grand wizard of the Ku Klux Klan. Some explicitly backed his Democratic opponent, the legendarily corrupt Edwin Edwards. Although the GOP certainly had a moral obligation to distance itself from a hate-monger, doing so was also in the party’s self-interest. Democrats, whose brand is viewed as too left-wing in five out of six Senate-battleground states, should be similarly wary of fanatics trying to infiltrate their ranks.

Open letters aren’t enough. If the Democrats want to root out the party within their party, they will need to stop indulging the DSA’s constant, passive-aggressive attempts to undermine them. This would require prohibiting DSA members from joining their party and throwing out the DSA members already honeycombed within it. Four decades ago, Labour’s Neil Kinnock expelled the extremist faction burrowing within his beloved party. If Democrats want to rescue their party, they may need to do the same.

entries.title: Trump Is Caught in the Trap He Set
entries.summary: The war in Iran has dealt a serious blow to the idea that the president can slip out of any bind easily.
entries.content:
The war in Iran has dealt a serious blow to the idea that the president can slip out of any bind easily.

This is an edition of The Atlantic Daily, a newsletter that guides you through the biggest stories of the day, helps you discover new ideas, and recommends the best in culture. Sign up for it here.

You could, if you were so inclined, tell a story of the Donald Trump era via memes. Pepe the Frog, “This Is Fine” Dog, “Covfefe,” Leopards Eating People’s Faces—things of that nature. One of the essential examples would be an October 2016 Twitter post by Jesse Farrar, parodying the enduring belief that the walls were closing in on Trump:

Well, I’d like to see ol Donny Trump wriggle his way out of THIS jam!

*Trump wriggles his way out of the jam easily*

Ah! Well. Nevertheless,

The conviction that Trump was on the brink of final collapse remained popular in some quarters for years after Farrar’s post—as anyone who remembers the fevered anticipation around Special Counsel Robert Mueller’s report and the subsequent letdown can attest—but at some moment, the tweet transformed from a riposte to naive pundits into a shorthand for the strangeness of American politics.

During the previous decade, Trump weathered a series of scandals that would likely have been career-ending for any other politician: the Access Hollywood tape, in which he boasted that he would “grab” women “by the pussy” without consent; the firing of then–FBI Director James Comey; his indiscreet sharing of intelligence with Russian officials in the Oval Office; an impeachment for attempting to extort Ukraine; an egregious approach to COVID-19, followed by a bout with the disease that reportedly nearly killed him; a series of attempts to steal the 2020 presidential election, culminating in the January 6 insurrection; his absconding with sensitive documents to Mar-a-Lago after his term ended; and 34 felony convictions. Despite all of this, Trump returned to the White House in January 2025. The notion of his invincibility became canon. Perhaps Trump himself came to believe it.

But the war in Iran has dealt a serious blow to the idea that Trump can slip out of any bind easily. Over the past three days, three American service members died in that conflict: two in Jordan, where Iranian missiles struck a military base, and a third in Iraq, killed by debris during the intentional destruction of an Iranian drone. The official American death toll in the Iran war now sits at 17.

That number is low—still less, for example, than the toll from the 1983 invasion of Grenada—although the military appears to have been keeping quiet just how many troops have been injured. As my colleague Tom Nichols wrote over the weekend, the president’s limp attempt at consolation (“It’s a very sad thing,” he said) is especially dismaying because “Trump has offered no comprehensive explanation about why his administration is ordering U.S. military personnel to put their lives on the line.”

And the death toll may begin to rise, because Trump appears to be on the verge of being drawn into a larger war. As the defense analyst Brynn Tannehill wrote in The Atlantic last week, the president faces only bad choices. The United States could effectively cede control of the Strait of Hormuz to Iran, accepting a fee system that breaks global precedent and centuries of American policy and granting a hostile power the opportunity to close an important shipping lane at any future moment. Or the White House could embark on a major ground war, because fighting over the past few months has shown the limitations of relying on air power. That would be politically hazardous, likely lead to more American deaths, and suck the U.S. into a long-running presence in the region, and it might still fail. Meanwhile, the conflict threatens to grow even larger as Iran’s Houthi allies in Yemen threaten to close some traffic to the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, which connects the Red Sea to the Gulf of Aden and the Indian Ocean.

Deciding what to do is hard when you don’t know what you want. Trump has vacillated over whether the point of the war is to prevent Iran from acquiring nuclear weapons and whether the war has already achieved that. He’s offered different answers about whether one goal is regime change, and whether that has been achieved. He has suggested that the purpose is to keep the Strait of Hormuz open, even though it was open before the war and even though that may no longer be manageable. The administration has also struggled to explain to skeptical members of Congress why it needs more war funding.

In short, Trump’s misadventure in Iran is a story of hubris. The president didn’t bother to plan or consider the dangers before he started a war, and now it has spiraled to a point where even he seems unable to find a way to wriggle out of a jam. For the first time in a very long time—to some extent, the first time in his life—Trump appears to be facing the consequences of his actions.

These circumstances might give Trump’s critics and opponents a chance to feel some delicious schadenfreude if the results of the blunder were not so terrible. The costs are measured not only in the blood of American service members but also in the deaths of Iranian civilians and of merchant mariners trying to sail through the Persian Gulf, in the damage to the global economy, and in the erosion of American alliances and a long-standing global order. Being confronted with a snare that he can’t escape must be unpleasant for Trump, but people around the globe will face misery because of it.

Related:

Here are four new stories from The Atlantic:

Today’s News

  • The Pentagon confirmed that dozens of U.S. service members were injured in three previously undisclosed Iranian strikes on bases in the Middle Eastbefore the attack on Friday that killed two American soldiers. A military official said those earlier attacks had not been publicly announced because the military does not routinely disclose injuries, particularly when troops quickly return to duty, and cited operational security concerns.
  • A man was taken into custody after authorities say he threw an incendiary device outside a federal building in New York City, setting a fire that caused minor injuries to three people. An investigator said he was carrying items including anti-ICE material and a pellet gun.
  • The FDA said it is continuing to investigate Taylor Farms, the produce company allegedly linked to the multistate cyclosporiasis outbreak, a day after it said that the company’s lettuce had produced a false-positive test. Taylor Farms’ voluntary recall of shredded iceberg lettuce remains in effect.

Dispatches

  • The Wonder Reader: As young people struggle to sit through even an hour of a film,modern blockbusters are getting longer, Isabel Fattal writes.

Explore all of our newsletters here.

Evening Read

The Wingman

By Mark Leibovich

“My wife, Gabby, was shot in the head.”

Obviously, I knew this. Senator Mark Kelly’s wife, the former congresswoman Gabby Giffords, was shot in the head and grievously wounded in a mass shooting outside a Tucson Safeway in 2011. It was one of the most notorious acts of political violence in America.

In several recent conversations with Kelly, however, I was struck by how often and how bluntly he repeated these exact words. Kelly, a retired Navy pilot and former NASA astronaut, seems to appreciate the curt repetition of the statement: My wife was shot in the head. It captures how stark and defining this incident was, and continues to be, in his life.

More From The Atlantic

Culture Break

Reflect. The World Cup could not be Americanized, Sally Jenkins argues. Spain’s 1–0 win over Argentina demonstrated all of the ways that soccer is still foreign in the United States.

Watch. James Parker on why Widow’s Bay (streaming on Apple TV) is the show of the summer.

Rafaela Jinich contributed to this newsletter.

entries.title: The ‘Hair-Dryer Incident’ Is Just the Start
entries.summary: A new, gonzo style of fraud could be an existential threat to prediction markets.
entries.content:
A new, gonzo style of fraud could be an existential threat to prediction markets.

There are worse things you could do than drive to a Parisian airport and mess around with a temperature sensor on the airfield—for only a few minutes! But why do that at all?

To make money, maybe. This is the scheme that some mysterious person (or persons) reportedly carried out in April to win a bet on the prediction-market platform Polymarket, which allows people to wager on the highest temperature in Paris on any given day, not unlike weather derivatives. The popular version of the story says that the shadowy figure warmed the sensor with a hair dryer—a photo showing this turned out to be AI generated—but it could have been anything. A lighter, a hand warmer.

By artificially boosting the number on the sensor, this person won about $20,000. The French police are investigating because you can’t trespass at the airport or tamper with its equipment, but Polymarket apparently didn’t take the winnings back. (It did change the airport that it relies on for Paris-temperature readings; the French government, however, blocked access to the platform this week.)

Over the past couple of years, plenty of concern has been expressed about how prediction markets may enable—and create the incentive for—insider trading. Some of the many people who have wagered on markets corresponding to when a war would start, when a regime would change, or even who would be at Taylor Swift’s wedding may actually have known the outcome before it happened. “Mention markets,” which allow participants to bet on words that a public figure might use in a speech, or how long the speech will be, can obviously be gamed by people close to the figure in question—just last week, ABC News reported that a teleprompter operator at the White House has made more than $100,000 by using Kalshi to wager on President Trump’s speeches. (“The White House has strict ethics guidelines that we expect all staffers and officials to follow,” a White House spokesperson told the outlet, while confirming that the employee had been placed on leave.)

The issues with insider trading are clear: It’s illegal, for starters, and the platforms ban it, anyway. (Although insider trading also arguably serves the purpose of prediction markets, which is to channel the “wisdom of the crowd” to forecast the event most likely to happen.) People can also easily identify and avoid the markets most susceptible to it. But what about the hair-dryer sort of trading, when an apparent outsider changes the course of events to cash in on a given outcome? “I think of it as probably a bigger issue or problem than insider trading would be,” Vincent Grégoire, a professor of finance at HEC Montréal, told me. The Parisian-airport incident didn’t hurt anybody, but future ones might. (You can imagine a bunch of ways in which someone might create a dangerous situation while, for example, attempting to delay an airplane.)

Some experts call this type of fraud “manipulation of the physical world”—a riff on market manipulation. I asked others for catchier ideas. Joshua Mitts, a professor at Columbia Law School, wasn’t sure that manipulation was the right word, because it has a specific definition for financial markets, but he still kept saying it accidentally as we spoke. I agree that the term is a little lacking. Manipulating the physical world is what I do every moment of every day as I breathe and walk. I would call this type of fraud “outsider trading.”

Outsider trading is also against platform rules. Polymarket and Kalshi both prohibit any type of market manipulation. When I reached out to Polymarket to ask about what had happened in Paris, a spokesperson relayed a statement to me from Olivia Chalos, its deputy chief legal officer, who wrote that Polymarket “maintains monitoring, review, enforcement, and reporting processes to address potential violations.”

But it’s obviously a bit of a whack-a-mole situation. The U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission recently proposed more federal rules for prediction-market platforms to ease the problem. One of the big suggestions was to prohibit the listing of markets that would “create perverse financial incentives”—for instance, markets on wildfires or a market on whether a famous ape named Little Joe would escape from a zoo (for a second time). Polymarket includes a note on that market explaining which law a person would be breaking if they attempted to free the gorilla themselves, and that they could receive a life sentence in prison for doing so. That may have been the first time a prediction-market platform has featured such a warning, the Bloomberg columnist Matt Levine noted in a May newsletter, “but I have not done a comprehensive survey and I doubt it’ll be the last.”

The experts I spoke with said that the most appealing markets for this kind of trickery would rely on a single number or simple, discrete outcome that a normal person could cheaply affect—the temperature reading in Paris, for example. Caleb Davies, an internet-famous prediction-market trader, highlighted another when he wrote about his experience losing thousands of dollars betting on Spotify’s streaming charts, which scammers had apparently figured out how to game by artificially juicing how many times a given song was played. Davies says the experience led him to an epiphany. He’s no longer just betting on the data, he said: “I’m also betting that nobody is going to mess with it.” And that’s a bad bet.

Davies posted a screenshot to X to show that he’d removed about $250,000 from his Kalshi account, and he says he’s stopped using it entirely. “I love prediction markets,” he told me. “I’d love to keep playing in them. But right now, both platforms are garbage, I think.” When I contacted the company for comment, the Kalshi spokesperson Jacki McGavick said that Kalshi “takes market manipulation or fraudulent activity as seriously as any federally regulated financial exchange” and is investigating the Spotify incident.

For the sake of conversation, Davies scrolled through Kalshi while we were on the phone, looking for opportunities for manipulation. Spotify charts were a good one, he knew. Rotten Tomatoes scores would be better (more money), though a little harder to game. (Nevertheless, New York magazine’s Matt Stieb figured out how to do it.) I pointed out to him that a market was open for the timing of this year’s first human case of screwworm in the United States. You could expose yourself to some infected livestock. But he wasn’t interested. “I think I’d just rather not make money than do that,” he said. “I’ve read about that—it doesn’t sound very appealing.”

Right now, the platforms—which are very much in advertising mode—carry a lot of markets that don’t have a ton of trading volume. They want as many as they can to attract new users and generate revenue. Also, a lot of them are funny: Most people probably recognize that it’s a waste of money to wager on whether Little Joe the gorilla will escape, but it’s fun to talk about. As more outsider-trading stories surface, though, people will likely be more hesitant to participate in markets that seem easy to rig. This problem, even more than insider trading, may start to limit what Polymarket and Kalshi can offer.

There are plenty of things the platforms could do to intervene in outsider trading, Joshua Della Vedova, an associate professor of finance at the University of San Diego’s business school, told me. They could limit the size of bets in markets in which the result would be easy to rig. They could look for suspicious trades using some of the same methods that detect insider trading. His preferred method looks for “excess accuracy.” If someone bets that an Olivia Rodrigo song is going to hit No. 1, that makes sense—but if they also specifically bet “no” on Drake and Charli XCX and Bad Bunny, then they’re demonstrating a suspicious amount of confidence. That confidence could come from having an inside line, or it could come from using bots to spike streams artificially. Platforms could also disallow markets that are resolved by checking a single number from a single source, he suggested. That would have solved the issue at the Paris airport, for example, and it’s not as if Polymarket couldn’t have used an aggregation of weather data, rather than just one reading.

Without some change, the platforms may continue to send ripples through reality—beyond just the goofy markets that they choose to list. A Wall Street Journal report found that Polymarket had been encouraging people to share videos with faked versions of their Polymarket dashboards, showing made-up earnings on imaginary bets that never happened. Weirder to me were the staged viral clips through which Polymarket and Kalshi looped themselves into the New York Knicks’ run to their first NBA Championship in more than 50 years.

In one of those clips, a purported Knicks fan outside Madison Square Garden yelled, apparently improvisationally, “My mayor Muslim, my bagels Jewish, my Christian Dior, Knicks in four.” Later, when it turned out that he hadn’t made this up at all—someone had posted it as a TikTok comment weeks before and parts of the rhyme were even older—and that the mic he’d yelled into had been Kalshi branded, the whole thing a Kalshi stunt, nobody seemed to care. The chant became real. It was everywhere.

The platforms pitch themselves as truth machines, which could mean a couple of different things. Are they finding it or making it? At some point, it may be difficult to identify the difference between reflecting our reality and creating a new one—or doing both.
